title: Internal wall insulation: choosing the right system
subtitle: PIR dry lining, stud-wall mineral wool, and the case for external insulation
excerpt: Solid walls (Victorian terraces, pre-1920 construction) have no cavity to fill. Internal wall insulation adds 70-150 mm to the inside face and can bring a 2.1 W/m²·K solid brick wall to below 0.5 W/m²·K. The three main systems differ in cost, depth loss, and thermal bridging risk.
sourceNotes: LETI (London Energy Transformation Initiative) internal wall insulation guidance 2021. BRE BR 443 solid wall U-value reference. EST solid wall insulation guidance. Historic England advice on insulating traditional buildings.

content.text: A solid brick wall (215 mm) has a U-value of 2.1 W/m²·K. Achieving the Part L target of 0.30 W/m²·K from inside requires around 90 mm of
(lambda 0.022) or 130 mm of mineral wool (lambda 0.035). Every millimetre of insulation depth comes at the cost of floor area, a 90 mm system on a 4 × 5 m room removes 1.7 m² of floor space.
System 1: PIR boards bonded directly to the wall (dry lining)

Foil-faced PIR boards are bonded to the wall using dabs of adhesive and a continuous perimeter bead. The board face is usually 12.5 mm plasterboard bonded to the PIR. Total thickness: 62-122 mm depending on board depth. This system has the lowest

so achieves the target U-value with the least depth loss. The main risk is condensation between the PIR and the cold original wall, the adhesive dabs must be air-tight to prevent air movement in that gap.
System 2: stud wall with mineral wool

A 70-100 mm metal or timber stud frame is built off the wall, filled with mineral wool batts, and faced with plasterboard. Deeper than PIR for the same U-value, and the timber or metal studs form regular
every 400-600 mm. Lower material cost than PIR, but the stud frame takes an additional 25-50 mm over a direct-bond system.

Insulation boards fixed to the outside face, rendered or clad over. This avoids all internal space loss and eliminates floor-joist bridging at the wall base. Costs significantly more than internal systems ( for a typical semi). Requires planning permission in conservation areas and for listed buildings. Considered the higher-performance option because it wraps the structure uniformly without creating cold spots at floor and ceiling junctions.
Available depth: PIR if every millimetre matters; stud wall if cost is the priority.
Planning: external insulation needs consent on listed buildings and in conservation areas. Internal insulation does not.
Thermal bridging at junctions: with internal insulation, floor joists set into the wall make cold spots. They are hard to close without cutting back floors. Plan for this at the detail design stage.
Disruption: internal work means moving skirting, sockets, radiators, and window boards. You do this in every room you treat.
